sections.ECG Monitoring: Indications and Limitati: Continuous ECG monitoring is mandatory in three categories of ICU patient: those with known or suspected cardiopulmonary disease (e.g. ischaemic heart disease, past arrhythmia, cardiothoracic surgery, advanced heart failure), those exposed to a pro-arrhythmic environment (electrical injury, invasive cardiac procedures, acute neurological disease, acute drug/metabolic toxicity, specific endocrine disease, risk of profound bradycardia, severe electrolyte disturbance), and those with a history of unexplained collapse (syncope). By definition, virtually all ICU patients require monitoring. Recognised limitations: difficult lead positioning (e.g. burns), movement/muscle artifact producing unreliable tracings, single-lead monitoring missing P waves if axis changes or missing ST changes outside the monitored territory, monitoring difficulty in prone patients, imperfect sensitivity/specificity of automated arrhythmia-detection software, and rare diathermy interference. A disconnected ECG lead can transmit 50 Hz mains interference producing an appearance indistinguishable from VF; always verify the rhythm is real (check two other leads, palpate pulse, check arterial or pulse-oximetry waveform) before treating. Pre-emptive application of anterior adhesive defibrillation pads before turning a patient prone facilitates rapid defibrillation without repositioning delay. A 12-lead ECG allows diagnosis of the nature and origin of almost any myocardial ischaemia or arrhythmia (right ventricular infarction is a recognised exception, needing right-sided leads); serial 12-lead ECGs may be required as situations evolve.
sections.General Principles of ECG Recording: The surface ECG records the heart's changing electrical potential as it depolarises and repolarises. Each lead represents net electrical activity in one region: activity directed toward the lead produces an upward (positive) deflection, activity directed away produces a downward (negative) deflection. Standard recording speed is 25 mm/sec, on paper composed of 5 mm 'large' squares (0.2 sec each), each subdivided into five 1 mm 'small' squares (0.04 sec each). Leads are grouped into the standard limb leads (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF) and the chest leads (V1-V6).
sections.Normal Cardiac Conduction: The sinoatrial (SA) node lies in the subepicardium at the right atrium/superior vena cava junction, has extensive autonomic innervation, and is supplied by the SA nodal artery (from the RCA in ~55% of people, from the left circumflex in ~45%). The atrioventricular (AV) node is a subendocardial structure within the interatrial septum, also extensively autonomically innervated, supplied by the AV nodal artery (a distal RCA branch in 90-95% of people). The His bundle forms from Purkinje fibres emerging from the distal AV node, running through the membranous then muscular septum before dividing into bundle branches; it has sparse autonomic innervation and dual blood supply from the AV nodal artery and septal branches of the LAD. The bundle branches (right bundle running to the anterior papillary muscle base; left bundle usually dividing into anterior and posterior hemibundles) have little autonomic innervation and extensive blood supply from both RCA and LCA. Normal conduction starts at the SA node, spreads through the atria, is delayed at the AV node (the only normal electrical connection between atria and ventricles, allowing atrial contribution to ventricular filling and protecting against VF during rapid atrial rates), then proceeds via the His-Purkinje system to activate the ventricles. All cardiac tissue retains automaticity, with intrinsic rate decreasing progressively down the conducting hierarchy (SA node fastest, ventricular myocardium slowest). QRS widening occurs with bundle branch block, accessory-pathway pre-excitation (e.g. WPW), ventricular-origin tachycardia, ventricular escape rhythms, or ventricular pacing — but NOT with first-degree AV block, which prolongs the PR interval without affecting QRS morphology.
sections.ECG Leads and Cardiac Territory: The standard limb leads view the heart from different angles around a hexaxial reference system: leads I and aVL 'look at' the left lateral surface, a territory supplied by the left coronary artery; leads II, III, and aVF are the inferior leads, viewing the heart from below in a territory supplied predominantly by the right coronary artery; lead aVR looks at the high right border of the heart. The chest (precordial) leads view the heart in the horizontal plane: V1 and V2 look at the anterior surface of the heart, including the right ventricle; V3 and V4 look at the septal area; V5 and V6 look at the lateral territory. A 12-lead ECG combining both lead groups allows fairly complete territorial localisation of ischaemia or arrhythmia origin, with right ventricular infarction as a recognised exception requiring additional right-sided leads (e.g. V4R).

questions.question: A 65-year-old man presents with an acute inferior STEMI (ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF). His admission ECG also shows complete (third-degree) atrioventricular block with a ventricular escape rhythm at 38 bpm. What is the most likely anatomical explanation for the AV block?
options: A: Occlusion of the LAD artery disrupting His-Purkinje blood supply from its septal branches
B: Spasm of the left circumflex artery causing left posterior fascicular block
C: Ischaemia of the SA node via the left circumflex artery causing retrograde propagation of block
D: Occlusion of the RCA interrupting blood supply to the AV node via the AV nodal artery, which arises from the RCA in 90-95% of the population
E: Extension of anterior infarction to the interventricular septum, disrupting the common His bundle at the point where it divides into bundle branches
questions.explanation: The AV nodal artery is a distal branch of the right coronary artery in 90-95% of the population. Inferior STEMI typically results from RCA occlusion; ischaemia of the AV node via the AV nodal artery is the dominant mechanism of AV block in inferior MI — hence 'AV block = inferior MI until proven otherwise'. This is physiologically distinct from bundle branch block, which complicates anterior MI (LAD occlusion threatening the LAD-dependent septal branches of the His-Purkinje system). Describing LAD disruption of septal His-Purkinje supply is the mechanism for bundle branch block in anterior MI, not for AV nodal block in inferior MI — the near-miss here confuses the two distinct MI-territory conduction complications.
questions.topic: AV-nodal-artery RCA inferior-MI complete-heart-block conduction-anatomy

questions.question: A patient with severe ARDS is placed in the prone position for 16-hour proning. During the session, the ECG monitor shows ventricular fibrillation. Attempts to defibrillate are delayed because the patient requires repositioning before anterior defibrillation pads can be placed. What precaution should have been taken before turning the patient prone?
options: A: A second continuous ECG monitor should have been attached to detect arrhythmias earlier
B: The prone position should be avoided altogether in any patient with a prior cardiac history, given the arrhythmia risk it introduces
C: Anterior adhesive defibrillation pads should have been applied to the chest before turning the patient prone
D: A prophylactic transvenous pacing wire should have been inserted before proning
E: A post-proning 12-lead ECG should have been obtained to identify evolving arrhythmias
questions.explanation: The PACT module explicitly describes this clinical scenario: a prone-ventilated ARDS patient developing VF with subsequent delay in defibrillation while the patient was turned supine. The recommended precaution is pre-emptive application of anterior adhesive defibrillation pads ('stick-on pads') before prone positioning, allowing rapid defibrillation without repositioning. This is a practical patient safety measure for any patient undergoing prone ventilation. The prone position is not contraindicated in patients with cardiac history — its survival benefit in severe ARDS is well established.
questions.topic: prone-ventilation VF defibrillation pads patient-safety ARDS ECG-monitoring

questions.question: A nurse urgently calls you to bay 3: the ECG monitor shows apparent ventricular fibrillation. On arrival, the patient is conscious, talking, and alert. One ECG lead is visibly disconnected from the skin electrode. The invasive arterial line shows a normal pulsatile waveform at 74 bpm. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: A: Obtain urgent 12-lead ECG before any intervention
B: Deliver immediate unsynchronised defibrillation at 200J — do not delay treatment
C: Commence chest compressions immediately, call the resuscitation team, and prepare the defibrillator and airway equipment for advanced life support
D: Administer IV amiodarone 300 mg immediately
E: Reattach the disconnected lead, confirm the rhythm on two other leads — likely mains interference at 50 Hz
questions.explanation: A disconnected ECG lead transmits mains electrical interference at 50 Hz (UK/EU) producing an ECG appearance indistinguishable from VF. The arterial waveform showing normal pulsatile flow at 74 bpm conclusively excludes VF — a patient in VF has no cardiac output. The correct action is always to verify the rhythm is real: check leads, confirm on two other leads, and assess the patient clinically. Defibrillating a patient who is NOT in VF can itself cause VF. This is a critical patient safety lesson: never act on ECG monitoring alone without confirming the patient's clinical status.
questions.topic: ECG-artifact mains-interference 50Hz VF-mimic patient-safety disconnected-lead

questions.question: On a standard ECG printed at 25 mm/sec, a clinician measures the PR interval as spanning 4 small squares. How long is this PR interval and what does it indicate?
options: A: 0.80 seconds — this would represent complete AV dissociation
B: 0.08 seconds — shortened PR, consistent with accessory pathway conduction
C: 0.40 seconds — markedly prolonged, indicating severe first-degree AV block
D: 0.16 seconds — within normal limits
E: 0.04 seconds — markedly shortened, suggesting pre-excitation
questions.explanation: At standard speed (25 mm/sec), each small square (1 mm) represents 0.04 seconds. Therefore, 4 small squares = 4 × 0.04 = 0.16 seconds — a normal PR interval (normal range 0.12-0.20 seconds, or 3-5 small squares). Each large square (5 mm) represents 0.2 seconds. Reading 4 LARGE squares instead of 4 small squares (4 × 0.2 sec = 0.8 sec) is the near-miss here — a clinically significant error that could lead to misdiagnosis of profound first-degree AV block from what is actually a normal PR interval. Accurate ECG measurement requires internalising these basic calibration values.
questions.topic: ECG-paper-speed PR-interval measurement small-squares large-squares calibration

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT cause QRS complex widening on the ECG?
options: A: Conduction through an accessory atrioventricular pathway (Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome)
B: First-degree atrioventricular block
C: Ventricular paced complexes
D: Left bundle branch block
E: Ventricular tachycardia originating in the ventricular myocardium
questions.explanation: First-degree AV block causes prolongation of the PR interval (delayed AV nodal conduction time) but does NOT widen the QRS — ventricular activation still proceeds normally through the His-Purkinje system once the impulse exits the AV node. QRS widening occurs when ventricular activation is abnormal or delayed: bundle branch block, pre-excitation via an accessory pathway bypassing the AV node, tachycardia originating in ventricular myocardium requiring slow myocardium-to-myocardium spread, and ventricular pacing all produce wide QRS. First-degree AV block is the near-miss here — it is a common finding and the temptation is to associate any 'block' with widening, but this one affects timing, not QRS morphology.
questions.topic: QRS-widening BBB WPW VT ventricular-pacing first-degree-AVB PR-interval

questions.question: A patient undergoes primary PCI for an acute inferior STEMI (dominant RCA territory). Post-procedure, he develops persistent sinus bradycardia and episodic sinus arrest requiring temporary pacing. Which anatomical fact most directly explains this complication?
options: A: The His bundle receives blood from septal branches of the LAD, which forms collateral communication with the RCA territory in the specific context of inferior myocardial infarction
B: Inferior MI causes diaphragmatic irritation leading to vagal sinus suppression
C: The SA nodal artery arises from the left circumflex artery in the majority of patients, causing sinus arrest when the LCx is involved
D: The AV node is supplied by the RCA in 90-95% of patients, and RCA occlusion causes AVN ischaemia
E: The SA nodal artery arises from the RCA in approximately 55% of people, making sinus node dysfunction a recognised complication of RCA territory infarction
questions.explanation: The SA nodal artery arises from the RCA in ~55% of people and from the left circumflex artery in ~45%. When inferior MI is caused by proximal RCA occlusion, the SA nodal artery may be compromised, causing sinus node ischaemia and dysfunction (bradycardia, sinus arrest). This is distinct from — and may coexist with — AV node ischaemia via the AV nodal artery (also primarily RCA). Reversing the proportions and claiming the LCx supplies the SA node in the majority is the near-miss: LCx supplies the SA node only in a minority (~45%), not the majority. Correctly describing AV node anatomy explains AV block, not sinus node dysfunction, so that option answers the wrong clinical question.
questions.topic: SA-nodal-artery RCA inferior-MI sinus-bradycardia sinus-arrest blood-supply

questions.question: A 32-year-old presents with palpitations and is found to have a supraventricular tachycardia at 215 bpm. IV adenosine is given to block the AV node. Beyond slowing the ventricular rate, what are the two principal physiological roles of the AV node that adenosine exploits?
options: A: Serving as the primary backup pacemaker after SA node failure, and additionally filtering out any retrograde impulses originating from ectopic ventricular foci during tachyarrhythmias
B: Delaying conduction from atria to ventricles (allowing atrial contribution to ventricular filling) AND protecting the ventricles from excessively rapid impulses that could cause VF
C: Accelerating His-Purkinje conduction and preventing ventricular escape rhythms during fast atrial rates
D: Delaying conduction through the His bundle and blocking atrial activation during tachycardia
E: Generating the dominant pacemaker impulse for the ventricles and providing a pathway for retrograde atrial activation
questions.explanation: The AV node serves two fundamental physiological roles: (1) it delays conduction between atria and ventricles, allowing time for the atrial component of ventricular filling (the 'atrial kick', particularly important in diastolic dysfunction); (2) it protects the ventricle from excessively rapid impulses during atrial tachyarrhythmias (e.g. AF or atrial flutter), preventing rates sufficient to cause VF. It is the ONLY normal electrical connection between atria and ventricles. AVN-blocking agents (adenosine, verapamil, diltiazem, β-blockers) exploit both these properties therapeutically. The AV node can serve as a backup pacemaker, but this is not one of its two principal roles and is a distractor mixing a true but secondary AV-node property into the answer.
questions.topic: AV-node physiology delay ventricular-filling VF-protection adenosine SVT

questions.question: A patient presents with an acute anterior STEMI and new left bundle branch block. Coronary angiography confirms proximal LAD occlusion. Which anatomical fact explains why LAD occlusion can disrupt His-Purkinje conduction?
options: A: The right bundle branch is exclusively supplied by the RCA; left bundle block in anterior MI reflects only myocardial oedema
B: The His bundle receives blood exclusively from the left circumflex artery in the setting of anterior MI
C: The His bundle receives blood exclusively from the AV nodal artery (an RCA branch), which communicates with the LAD via septal collaterals
D: The His bundle and bundle branches receive blood partly from septal branches of the LAD, making them vulnerable to LAD territory ischaemia
E: The bundle branches are entirely avascular, making them vulnerable to ischaemia from any coronary territory
questions.explanation: The His bundle has a dual blood supply: the AV nodal artery (distal RCA branch) AND septal branches of the LAD artery. The bundle branches also receive extensive supply from both RCA and LCA. LAD occlusion therefore threatens the LAD-dependent component of His-Purkinje blood supply, causing bundle branch block in anterior MI. This dual supply makes the His bundle more resistant to total ischaemia than the AV node (which relies predominantly on the single AV nodal artery), explaining why complete AV block is less common in anterior MI than in inferior MI. Bundle branch block in anterior STEMI is a high-risk marker requiring close monitoring.
questions.topic: His-bundle blood-supply LAD septal-branches LBBB anterior-MI dual-supply

questions.question: Which of the following patients does NOT require continuous ECG monitoring based on established indications?
options: A: A 35-year-old with no cardiac history undergoing elective hernia repair under general anaesthesia
B: A patient with a history of unexplained syncopal episodes awaiting tilt-table testing
C: A patient 6 hours post primary PCI for anterior STEMI, currently on the cardiac ICU for ongoing haemodynamic observation
D: A patient with serum K+ 7.4 mmol/L being treated for hyperkalaemia
E: A patient 2 days post subarachnoid haemorrhage who developed new QTc prolongation
questions.explanation: A healthy 35-year-old with no cardiac history undergoing uncomplicated elective surgery has none of the three indications for ECG monitoring: no known cardiopulmonary disease, no pro-arrhythmic environment exposure, and no history of unexplained collapse. All others have clear indications: recent STEMI falls under known cardiopulmonary disease; severe hyperkalaemia is a severe electrolyte disturbance and thus a pro-arrhythmic environment; subarachnoid haemorrhage with QTc prolongation is acute neurological disease, also a pro-arrhythmic environment; and unexplained syncope is a history of unexplained collapse. The PACT module notes that by definition, virtually all ICU patients require ECG monitoring.
questions.topic: ECG-monitoring indications pro-arrhythmic cardiopulmonary-disease syncope electrolyte

questions.question: A 28-year-old is found to have Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome. His resting ECG shows a short PR interval, delta wave, and widened QRS. Which mechanism best explains these findings in the context of normal AV conduction physiology?
options: A: The accessory pathway conducts only in a retrograde direction, speeding atrial-to-ventricular conduction through the AV node
B: WPW results from anatomical duplication of the His bundle itself, creating a second, independent high-speed conduction route running parallel to the normal pathway through the septum
C: The delta wave represents delayed AV nodal conduction resulting from enhanced vagal tone in young athletes
D: The accessory pathway conducts impulses faster through the His-Purkinje system than the AV node normally would, thereby shortening overall QRS duration
E: The accessory pathway bypasses the AV node, directly activating ventricular myocardium and producing pre-excitation, which shortens the PR interval and produces the delta wave
questions.explanation: In WPW, an accessory atrioventricular pathway bypasses the AV node — the normally obligatory delay between atrial and ventricular activation. The result is pre-excitation: the ventricle near the pathway insertion is activated early, directly from the atrium via slow myocardium-to-myocardium conduction, before the main His-Purkinje activation wave arrives — producing the delta wave and widened QRS. The absence of AV nodal delay produces the short PR interval. The module specifically lists 'ventricular activation through an alternative AV pathway (e.g. an accessory or non-His-AV bundle)' as a cause of QRS widening. The clinical danger in WPW is AF conducting rapidly (and potentially causing VF) via the accessory pathway bypassing the AV node's protective rate-limiting function.
questions.topic: WPW accessory-pathway pre-excitation delta-wave AV-node-bypass QRS-widening

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a recognised limitation of single-lead continuous ECG monitoring in the ICU?
options: A: Automated arrhythmia detection software may lack both sensitivity and specificity
B: ST-segment changes in an ischaemic territory not represented by the monitored lead may be missed
C: A 12-lead ECG cannot be obtained in patients already connected to continuous ECG monitoring
D: Movement artifact and shivering can produce unreliable tracings and false arrhythmia alarms
E: P waves may be difficult to identify if the cardiac axis changes
questions.explanation: A 12-lead ECG can always be obtained in addition to — not instead of — continuous single-lead monitoring. The PACT module explicitly states that a 12-lead ECG 'should be obtained wherever possible' for definitive arrhythmia and ischaemia diagnosis. All other options are genuine, recognised limitations: single-lead monitoring may miss P waves if the axis changes, may fail to detect ischaemia in unmonitored territories, is susceptible to movement and muscle artifact, and relies on imperfect automated detection algorithms. The limitation of single-lead is spatial, not temporal — a 12-lead ECG overcomes the spatial limitation.
questions.topic: single-lead ECG-monitoring limitations 12-lead P-wave ischaemia artifact

questions.question: A patient develops complete (third-degree) AV block. The P-P interval is 80 bpm. The ventricular escape rhythm is 32 bpm with broad, bizarre QRS complexes unrelated to P waves. What does the morphology and rate of the escape rhythm indicate about the automaticity hierarchy of the conducting system?
options: A: The escape focus is in the His bundle, producing broad QRS at 40-60 bpm
B: The escape focus is in the ventricular myocardium — the lowest level of the automaticity hierarchy — producing broad QRS at an intrinsic rate of approximately 20-40 bpm
C: The escape focus is in the SA node, which is firing independently at its intrinsic rate
D: The escape focus is located in the AV node itself, which has an intrinsic firing rate of 60-80 bpm, concordant with the observed ventricular rate
E: The rate of 32 bpm indicates accelerated automaticity in a ventricular focus specifically due to catecholamine stimulation during the acute stress of complete heart block
questions.explanation: All cardiac tissues possess automaticity, but the intrinsic rate decreases progressively down the conducting hierarchy: SA node (60-100 bpm) → AV node/junction (40-60 bpm) → His-Purkinje (25-45 bpm) → ventricular myocardium (20-40 bpm). The broad, bizarre QRS complexes at 32 bpm indicate a ventricular escape focus — aberrant, slow myocardium-to-myocardium spread from a distal pacemaker. AV nodal/junctional escapes produce narrower QRS at higher rates (40-60 bpm). A ventricular escape rhythm at these rates is haemodynamically precarious and represents an urgent indication for transvenous pacing. This case illustrates why AV node dysfunction in inferior MI (RCA territory) requires close monitoring and pacing readiness.
questions.topic: automaticity-hierarchy ventricular-escape complete-AV-block QRS-morphology pacemaker-rates

questions.question: A 58-year-old presents with acute chest pain. The ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF, with reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL. Which coronary territory is most likely involved, and which additional leads would help assess for right ventricular extension?
options: A: Right coronary artery territory (inferior wall); right-sided leads such as V4R should be examined for right ventricular involvement
B: Left main coronary artery territory (global ischaemia); no additional right-sided leads are required to assess for right ventricular involvement in this pattern
C: Left anterior descending territory; leads V1-V2 should be examined for right ventricular involvement
D: Right coronary artery territory (anterior wall); leads V1-V2 should be examined for right ventricular involvement
E: Left circumflex territory (lateral wall); leads V5-V6 should be examined for right ventricular involvement
questions.explanation: Leads II, III, and aVF are the inferior leads, viewing the heart from below in a territory predominantly supplied by the right coronary artery; reciprocal ST depression in the lateral leads I and aVL (left coronary artery territory) supports this localisation rather than a primary lateral event. Since the RCA frequently also supplies the right ventricle, right ventricular extension is a recognised feature of inferior STEMI, but this is a recognised exception that a standard 12-lead ECG may not reliably show, requiring dedicated right-sided leads such as V4R. Describing an anterior or lateral vessel territory misattributes the inferior lead pattern, and dismissing the need for right-sided leads ignores the specific RV-infarction exception.
questions.topic: inferior-STEMI RCA-territory reciprocal-changes right-ventricular-infarction V4R lead-territory

questions.question: A 60-year-old develops acute chest pain. The ECG shows new ST elevation confined to leads V1 through V4, with no significant change in the inferior or lateral leads. Which territory and vessel does this pattern most likely represent?
options: A: Lateral territory; left circumflex artery
B: High right atrial territory; SA nodal artery
C: Anteroseptal territory; left anterior descending artery
D: Inferior territory; right coronary artery
E: Posterior territory; right coronary artery, seen here as reciprocal change
questions.explanation: Leads V1 and V2 look at the anterior surface of the heart, including the right ventricle, and leads V3 and V4 look at the septal area; ST elevation spanning V1-V4 therefore localises to the anteroseptal territory, classically supplied by the left anterior descending artery — the same vessel whose septal branches supply part of the His bundle, explaining the recognised association between extensive anterior infarction and bundle branch block. A lateral pattern would show changes in V5-V6, an inferior pattern would show changes in II, III, and aVF, and a posterior pattern would present as ST depression (not elevation) in the anterior chest leads as a reciprocal change.
questions.topic: anteroseptal-STEMI LAD chest-lead-territory V1-V4 His-bundle-blood-supply

questions.question: A 70-year-old woman with a 90% proximal RCA stenosis presents with acute inferior STEMI and Mobitz type II second-degree AV block on her admission ECG. Why is second-degree AV block a recognised complication of inferior (rather than anterior) MI in the majority of patients?
options: A: The AV nodal artery is a distal branch of the RCA in 90-95% of patients, making the AV node vulnerable to ischaemia in RCA territory infarction
B: Inferior MI causes diaphragmatic compression of the cardiac apex, mechanically impeding AV nodal conduction
C: The anterior wall of the left ventricle houses the majority of the His-Purkinje system, which becomes selectively damaged whenever ischaemia extends toward the inferior wall
D: Inferior MI triggers a vagal reflex causing reversible depression of AV node function
E: The left circumflex artery supplies the AV node in the majority of patients, and inferior MI typically involves the LCx
questions.explanation: The AV nodal artery arises from the distal RCA in 90-95% of the population. In the most common pattern of coronary anatomy, inferior MI from RCA occlusion compromises AV nodal blood supply, causing AV block. Anterior MI (LAD territory) more commonly causes bundle branch block from His-Purkinje ischaemia (LAD supplies septal branches to His bundle), rather than nodal AV block. Claiming the LCx supplies the AV node in the majority of patients is the critical near-miss: the LCx supplies the AV node only in left-dominant patients, a minority. This anatomy explains the PACT teaching principle: AV block complicating inferior MI is typically transient and often responds to atropine, while AV block complicating anterior MI is high-risk and often requires pacing.
questions.topic: AV-nodal-artery RCA inferior-MI AV-block coronary-anatomy Mobitz

questions.question: Regarding the anatomy and blood supply of the sinoatrial (SA) node:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Inferior MI caused by RCA occlusion can cause sinus node dysfunction when the SA nodal artery is a branch of the occluded RCA

statements.label: B
statements.text: The SA nodal artery arises from the right coronary artery in approximately 55% of people and from the left circumflex artery in approximately 45%

statements.label: C
statements.text: The SA node is located in the subepicardium at the junction of the right atrium and superior vena cava

statements.label: D
statements.text: The SA node has sparse autonomic innervation, making it relatively resistant to pharmacological modulation

questions.explanation: The subepicardial location at the RA-SVC junction is correct, as is the SA nodal artery arising from the RCA in ~55% and the LCx in ~45%. Claiming sparse autonomic innervation for the SA node is false: the SA node has EXTENSIVE autonomic innervation — this is why vagal tone (e.g. Valsalva, carotid sinus massage) and sympathetic stimulation so potently affect sinus rate. It is the His bundle and bundle branches that have sparse or little autonomic innervation. The RCA-occlusion link to sinus bradycardia and sinus arrest is also correct, explaining these as recognised complications of inferior MI when the SA nodal artery is RCA-derived.
questions.topic: SA-node anatomy blood-supply RCA LCx autonomic-innervation inferior-MI

questions.question: Regarding the anatomy and physiology of the atrioventricular (AV) node:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The AV nodal artery is a distal branch of the right coronary artery in 90-95% of the population

statements.label: B
statements.text: The AV node has sparse autonomic innervation, in contrast to the bundle branches which are extensively innervated

statements.label: C
statements.text: Under normal circumstances, the AV node is the only electrical connection between the atria and ventricles

statements.label: D
statements.text: The AV node is a subendocardial structure located within the interatrial septum

questions.explanation: The subendocardial location within the interatrial septum is correct, as is the AV nodal artery arising from the distal RCA in 90-95% of the population — the basis for AV block complicating inferior MI. Claiming sparse autonomic innervation for the AV node is false: the AV node has EXTENSIVE autonomic innervation, and this modulation of AV nodal conduction is clinically fundamental (vagal tone slows conduction; sympathetic activity speeds it). It is the His bundle and bundle branches that have sparse or little autonomic innervation. The AV node being the only normal electrical connection between atria and ventricles is also correct; accessory pathways (e.g. WPW) represent abnormal additional connections.
questions.topic: AV-node anatomy interatrial-septum RCA autonomic-innervation electrical-connection

questions.question: Regarding the His bundle and its clinical significance:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The His bundle receives blood from both the AV nodal artery (distal RCA branch) and septal branches of the LAD artery

statements.label: B
statements.text: The His bundle is formed by Purkinje fibres emerging from the distal AV node and runs through the membranous and then muscular interventricular septum before dividing into bundle branches

statements.label: C
statements.text: Bundle branch block complicating anterior MI reflects ischaemia of the His-Purkinje system due to LAD territory involvement of its septal blood supply

statements.label: D
statements.text: The His bundle has extensive autonomic innervation allowing rapid modulation of ventricular conduction velocity

questions.explanation: The His bundle anatomy and course as described is correct, as is its dual blood supply from both the AV nodal artery (RCA) and LAD septal branches, which makes it more resistant to ischaemia than the AV node (which relies predominantly on one source). Claiming extensive autonomic innervation is false: the His bundle has SPARSE autonomic innervation, in contrast to the SA node and AV node which are extensively innervated. LAD occlusion threatening the LAD-dependent septal blood supply to the His-Purkinje system is also correct, explaining why bundle branch block complicates anterior (not usually inferior) MI.
questions.topic: His-bundle anatomy Purkinje blood-supply LAD AV-nodal-artery autonomic bundle-branch-block

questions.question: Regarding causes of QRS complex widening on the ECG:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Ventricular paced complexes produce broad, abnormal QRS morphology due to non-His-Purkinje ventricular activation

statements.label: B
statements.text: First-degree AV block causes QRS widening due to prolonged conduction through the His-Purkinje system

statements.label: C
statements.text: Bundle branch block (damage to the conducting system) is a recognised cause of QRS widening

statements.label: D
statements.text: Conduction through an accessory AV pathway, as in Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome, causes QRS widening via pre-excitation

questions.explanation: Conducting system damage causing slow aberrant spread through ventricular myocardium and widening QRS is correct, as is pre-excitation via an accessory pathway bypassing the AV node and activating ventricular myocardium before the His-Purkinje system engages, producing the delta wave and widened QRS. Attributing QRS widening to first-degree AV block is false: it causes PR interval prolongation (delayed AV nodal conduction) but does NOT widen the QRS, since His-Purkinje activation is normal once the impulse passes the AV node. Ventricular pacing initiating activation from the pacing electrode in non-conducting myocardium and producing wide QRS is also correct — one of the five enumerated causes of QRS widening in the PACT module.
questions.topic: QRS-widening BBB WPW first-degree-AVB ventricular-pacing aberrant-conduction

questions.question: Regarding normal cardiac conduction and the automaticity hierarchy:

statements.label: A
statements.text: All areas of the heart have the capacity for automaticity; the intrinsic pacemaker rate decreases progressively from the SA node toward the ventricular myocardium

statements.label: B
statements.text: Normal conduction is initiated in the SA node, spreads through the atria, is delayed at the AV node, and then travels via the His-Purkinje system to activate the ventricles

statements.label: C
statements.text: The AV node delays conduction to allow the atrial contribution to ventricular filling and protects against ventricular fibrillation during rapid atrial arrhythmias

statements.label: D
statements.text: Because the His-Purkinje system has no automaticity below the level of the AV node, complete AV block results in ventricular standstill unless a pacemaker is inserted

questions.explanation: The standard conduction sequence described is correct, as are the two principal roles of the AV node and the automaticity hierarchy (SA node ~60-100 bpm > AV junction ~40-60 bpm > His-Purkinje ~25-45 bpm > ventricular myocardium ~20-40 bpm). Claiming the His-Purkinje system has no automaticity below the AV node is false: all cardiac areas, including the His-Purkinje system and ventricular myocardium, retain automaticity. Complete AV block therefore produces a ventricular escape rhythm — broad, slow, unreliable — rather than standstill, though this escape rhythm is haemodynamically compromised and unreliable, making pacing urgent.
questions.topic: conduction-pathway automaticity-hierarchy AV-node SA-node escape-rhythm His-Purkinje

questions.question: Regarding standard ECG recording principles:

statements.label: A
statements.text: If the net cardiac electrical activity is directed away from a lead electrode, the resulting ECG deflection will be predominantly positive (upward)

statements.label: B
statements.text: The standard limb leads consist of leads I, II, III, aVR, aVL, and aVF

statements.label: C
statements.text: Standard ECG recording speed is 25 mm/sec; each small square (1 mm) represents 0.04 seconds and each large square (5 mm) represents 0.2 seconds

statements.label: D
statements.text: If the net cardiac electrical activity is directed toward a lead electrode, the resulting ECG deflection will be predominantly positive (upward)

questions.explanation: The fundamental calibration of ECG paper that all clinicians must internalise is correct, as is net activity toward an electrode producing a positive (upward) deflection. Claiming that activity directed away from the electrode ALSO produces a positive deflection is false: activity away from the electrode produces a NEGATIVE (downward) deflection — the foundational principle of vector-based ECG interpretation, and confusing this direction-to-deflection relationship is a common source of ECG misinterpretation. Listing I, II, III, aVR, aVL, and aVF as the standard limb leads is also correct; chest leads V1-V6 form the second group.
questions.topic: ECG-recording speed squares calibration deflection-direction limb-leads

questions.question: Regarding practical problems and patient safety in ECG monitoring:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Before acting on a monitor showing ventricular fibrillation, the clinician should verify the rhythm by checking the patient's pulse, arterial waveform, or confirming on two other leads

statements.label: B
statements.text: Pre-emptive application of anterior adhesive defibrillation pads before prone positioning is recommended to facilitate rapid defibrillation without the delay of turning the patient supine

statements.label: C
statements.text: Automated arrhythmia detection software in current ECG monitoring systems has been demonstrated to have both high sensitivity and high specificity, making clinical ECG review unnecessary

statements.label: D
statements.text: A disconnected ECG lead can transmit mains electrical interference at 50 Hz, producing an ECG appearance indistinguishable from ventricular fibrillation

questions.explanation: Mains hum at 50 Hz from a disconnected lead mimicking VF is correct — a critical patient safety teaching point — as is always verifying the rhythm by clinical correlation before defibrillating. Claiming automated detection software has both high sensitivity and specificity is false: the PACT module explicitly states that arrhythmia detection software may lack both sensitivity and specificity, a recognised limitation of automated monitoring, so clinical review remains essential. Pre-emptive anterior defibrillation pads before prone positioning allowing immediate defibrillation without repositioning, as described in the PACT module anecdote, is also correct.
questions.topic: ECG-artifact 50Hz VF-mimic patient-safety prone-pads automated-detection software

questions.question: Regarding indications for continuous ECG monitoring in hospital settings:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Electrical injury requires ECG monitoring only if the patient develops symptoms immediately after the injury

statements.label: B
statements.text: Patients with severe electrolyte disturbances (e.g. hyperkalaemia, hypomagnesaemia) require ECG monitoring as they are in a pro-arrhythmic environment

statements.label: C
statements.text: Patients with a history of unexplained syncope require ECG monitoring to detect the responsible arrhythmia

statements.label: D
statements.text: Acute neurological disease, including subarachnoid haemorrhage and traumatic brain injury, is a recognised indication for ECG monitoring

questions.explanation: Severe electrolyte disturbance being explicitly listed as a pro-arrhythmic environment is correct, as is acute neurological disease (autonomic storms, catecholamine surges, neurogenic QTc prolongation in SAH) being a recognised pro-arrhythmic condition, and unexplained syncope forming the third category of ECG monitoring indication, used to capture the responsible arrhythmia. Restricting electrical injury monitoring to only symptomatic patients is false: electrical injury requires ECG monitoring regardless of whether symptoms are immediate, since arrhythmias can be delayed and the patient may appear well at first. Continuous monitoring is mandatory in electrical injury patients.
questions.topic: ECG-monitoring indications electrolyte neurological syncope electrical-injury delayed

questions.question: Regarding the 12-lead ECG and its role in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Right ventricular infarction is an example of a condition that may not be reliably diagnosed on a standard 12-lead ECG, requiring additional right-sided leads (e.g. V4R)

statements.label: B
statements.text: Continuous single-lead ECG monitoring provides equivalent diagnostic information to a 12-lead ECG and renders serial 12-lead ECGs unnecessary in monitored ICU patients

statements.label: C
statements.text: A 12-lead ECG allows diagnosis of the nature and origin of almost any myocardial ischaemia or arrhythmia — including VT originating from either the right or left ventricle

statements.label: D
statements.text: Serial 12-lead ECGs may be required in evolving clinical situations to track dynamic changes in ischaemia or arrhythmia

questions.explanation: The 12-lead ECG providing spatial resolution across all cardiac territories, essential for precise ischaemia localisation and arrhythmia characterisation, is correct. The PACT module specifically citing right ventricular infarction as an exception — where a standard 12-lead may not reveal it, necessitating right-sided leads — is also correct. Claiming single-lead monitoring provides equivalent diagnostic information is false: single-lead monitoring offers temporal resolution (continuous) but lacks spatial resolution, and cannot replace the 12-lead ECG for ischaemia diagnosis, arrhythmia origin, axis determination, or QTc measurement. Serial ECGs tracking evolving ischaemia, arrhythmia burden, and treatment response is also correct and an important clinical tool.
questions.topic: 12-lead-ECG RV-infarction right-sided-leads serial-ECG spatial-resolution single-lead

questions.question: Regarding the bundle branches and their anatomy:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The left bundle branch usually divides into a left anterior hemibundle and a left posterior hemibundle (and sometimes a third septal fascicle)

statements.label: B
statements.text: The bundle branches receive an extensive blood supply from both the right and left coronary arteries

statements.label: C
statements.text: The bundle branches have extensive autonomic innervation, allowing direct autonomic modulation of ventricular conduction velocity

statements.label: D
statements.text: The right bundle branch extends down the right side of the interventricular septum to the base of the anterior papillary muscle

questions.explanation: The right bundle anatomy as described is correct, as is the left bundle typically bifurcating into anterior and posterior hemibundles — the basis for 'hemiblocks' (left anterior and left posterior fascicular block). Claiming extensive autonomic innervation for the bundle branches is false: they have LITTLE autonomic innervation, unlike the SA node and AV node which are extensively innervated, making bundle branch conduction relatively autonomically independent and not easily modulated pharmacologically. The extensive dual blood supply from both RCA and LCA making the bundle branches relatively resistant to ischaemia from single-vessel disease is also correct — bilateral coronary disease is usually needed to cause bilateral bundle branch block.
questions.topic: bundle-branches right-bundle left-bundle hemibundle autonomic-innervation blood-supply

questions.question: Regarding the anatomical correlation between ECG leads and cardiac territory:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Leads V3 and V4 overlie the septal area of the heart.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Leads I and aVL primarily reflect electrical activity from the left lateral surface of the heart, a territory supplied by the left coronary artery.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Leads II, III, and aVF are the inferior leads, viewing the heart from below in a territory predominantly supplied by the right coronary artery.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Leads V1 and V2 primarily reflect the lateral wall of the left ventricle.

questions.explanation: Leads I and aVL looking at the left lateral surface (left coronary artery territory) is correct, as is leads II, III, and aVF being the inferior leads viewing the heart from below, predominantly right coronary artery territory. Leads V1 and V2 look at the anterior surface of the heart, including the right ventricle, not the lateral wall — the lateral territory is instead viewed by V5 and V6, making that claim false. Leads V3 and V4 overlying the septal area is correct, completing the standard chest-lead territory map alongside V1-V2 (anterior/RV) and V5-V6 (lateral).
questions.topic: lead-territory-map limb-leads chest-leads axis coronary-artery-correlation
